serving.natural_language_portion: One medium chicken pau weighs approximately 80 g and is a common single-serve portion at Singaporean hawker centres and dim sum stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Pau — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these have been retained as plausible estimates but confidence is reduced to 0.70 and documented accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun / Chinese steamed bun — analogous GI estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken pau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: Chinese steamed white bread/bun (mantou-style), which has a reported GI of approximately 65 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 26.54) / 100 ≈ 17.2, using an 80 g serving (26.54 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 378 mg/100 g combined with high sugar 10.31 g/100 g and low dietary fibre 1.84 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken pau to one small piece per occasion, avoid adding soy sauce or chilli sauce, and pair with high-potassium vegetables to offset the sodium load; the high sugar content (>10 g/100 g with fibre <2 g/100 g) further drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate 33.18 g/100 g with low dietary fibre 1.84 g/100 g and sugar 10.31 g/100 g producing a medium-to-high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict portion to half a pau (approximately 40 g), pair with a high-fibre or protein-rich side dish to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose, as the refined wheat dough and added sugar create a glycaemic load of approximately 17 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g, trans fat 0.07 g/100 g, cholesterol 36.67 mg/100 g, and sodium 378 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume chicken pau occasionally and in small portions, as the saturated fat and sodium content contribute to atherogenic and hypertensive risk; choosing a pau made with vegetable shortening rather than lard and requesting reduced-salt filling can modestly lower the cardiovascular burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken pau (鸡肉包; Pau Ayam) is a popular Singaporean Chinese steamed bun filled with seasoned minced chicken meat. The soft, pillowy white dough is made from wheat flour, yeast, sugar, and shortening, then wrapped around a savoury filling of minced chicken typically seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and aromatics such as ginger and spring onion. The bun is steamed until fluffy and slightly glossy on the outside. Chicken pau is a staple at Singaporean hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and convenience stores, commonly eaten as a breakfast item or snack. It is widely considered halal and is popular across all ethnic communities in Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 235 kcal, 8.87 g protein, 7.44 g fat (of which 4.0 g is saturated), 33.18 g carbohydrate, 10.31 g sugar, 1.84 g dietary fibre, and 378 mg sodium. The high sugar and low fibre content of the refined dough creates a medium glycaemic index (estimated ~65) and a glycaemic load of approximately 17 per standard 80 g serving, making portion control important for individuals with diabetes or hypertension. The dish is not suitable for low-sodium or low-sugar diets without modification.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) retained from input at reduced confidence 0.70 as they were attributed to a non-approved delivery platform source; these are plausible for this food type but not independently verified against an approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using Chinese steamed white bun as the closest match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for chicken pau are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. Micronutrients calcium (50 mg), iron (1.5 mg), cholesterol (36.67 mg), saturated fat (4.0 g), trans fat (0.07 g), and vitamin C (6.67 mg) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source (grabfood, confidence 0.70); these values are plausible for this food type and have been retained at reduced confidence pending verification against an approved source. The input record contained health_suitability entries for rheumatoid_arthritis referencing nightshade ingredients (tomato, potato, green chilli) and curry spices (turmeric, ginger, cumin, coriander) that are inconsistent with a standard chicken pau formulation; these claims have been removed as unsupported. Portion labels '1 plate' (550 g) and '1 serving of curry' (300 g) from the input are inconsistent with a standard chicken pau and have been replaced with a single defensible 80 g serving. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Chinese steamed white bun from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken pau was found.
